turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Responding to Feedback or a Performance Review

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Hi, yes, thank you. I got feedback from my supervisor. It’s not horrible, but I feel… eh… nervous, because I don’t know how to respond correctly without sounding defensive.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s a very mature concern. Feedback can feel personal even when it’s practical. Was it written, or said in a meeting?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: It was a meeting, but after they sent a short email with the points, so now it feels more official.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Okay, spoken and written. What were the main points? Let’s start with one positive thing.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: They said I’m friendly, customers like me, and I’m reliable. So I feel good about that. But then they said I need to be more “assertive” when it’s busy, and I don’t know what they want, because I don’t want to be rude.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Important nuance. Assertive doesn’t mean rude. Usually it means clear, confident, direct when needed. Did they give examples?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: They said when customers are waiting, I spend too long explaining, then the line becomes big. But I’m like, I’m trying to be helpful… so I felt confused.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That makes sense. Your intention is helpfulness, but the impact is slower service during peak time. Did you ask them what “assertive” looks like?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: No… in the moment my brain went blank. I was smiling, but inside I was like “oh no.”

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That happens a lot. You can still ask now, like a follow-up. Would you feel comfortable asking for one or two concrete examples?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Yes, but I don’t know how to say it without sounding like I’m challenging them.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: We can keep it collaborative. Like: “Thank you for the feedback, I want to improve. Could you share an example of what assertive communication looks like in busy periods?”

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: That sounds good. Also I want to explain I’m not slow because I don’t care, it’s because I’m trying to be polite. But maybe I should not explain too much?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Great self-awareness. You can briefly acknowledge intention, but focus on action: “I understand the need to keep service moving. I’ll practise being concise in busy times.”

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Concise, yes, that’s the word. Sometimes I talk too much because I want to sound friendly, and also because I’m thinking in my head and speaking at the same time, you know?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Very normal for multilingual speakers. Did they mention anything else?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: They also said “take more initiative.” And again I’m like, what initiative? In café job everything is routine. I don’t want to do something and it’s wrong.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Good question. Initiative can be small proactive things: restocking, wiping tables, checking who needs help, without being asked. Does that match your workplace?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Yes, it matches. I do some, but maybe not enough, or they didn’t notice. And I don’t like interrupting people.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Understandable. You can mention you’ll focus on visible proactive actions, especially between busy and quiet moments.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Okay. And should I reply to the email quickly? If I wait, it looks bad. If I reply too fast, my English is messy.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Reply within one or two days is ideal. Keep it simple. Draft it, leave it, reread.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Yes, I like that. Another thing, they said “confidence.” They said I should sound more confident with customers. But I’m confident inside, it’s just my accent. I mean, I have accent and then I feel less confident.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Important point. Accent is not lack of competence. Confidence can show through pace, clarity, short phrases. Want to practise a few set phrases for busy times?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Yes, please. Like short phrases I can repeat without thinking too much.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Perfect. Examples: “I’ll be right with you,” “Thanks for waiting,” “Let me confirm that,” “I can explain more in a moment.”

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Oh, I like “I can explain more in a moment.” It’s polite but it keeps the line moving.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Exactly. Do you want to respond by email, or in person?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Email is safer because I can think. In person I become nervous and I say too many things.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Great strategy. In the email: thank them, acknowledge key points, state actions, ask for examples of “assertive” and “initiative.”

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Yes. And can I ask for a check-in in some weeks, to see if I improve? Or is that too much?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s actually excellent. It shows commitment.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Okay. I feel much better now. I thought feedback means I’m failing, but now I see it’s like… adjustment.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Exactly. Quick check: positive feedback for friendliness/reliability, improvement points were assertiveness in busy times, initiative, confidence. You’ll reply with thanks, clarification request, and concrete actions, and ask for a follow-up check-in. Correct?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: Yes, correct. Thank you. I feel calmer, and like I have a plan instead of just feelings.
